claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

**First, let's talk about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is your child? 

- Are they **under 16 years old**?
- Are they **16 to 19 years old**?
- Or are they **20 or over**?

Please let me know the child's age (or age category) so we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually inquiring about two children - Alex, who's 12, and Blake, who's 6.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you with both Alex (12 years old) and Blake (6 years old).

Since both Alex and Blake are under 16 years old, they potentially qualify for Child Benefit based on age. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now, the next question:

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given their ages (12 and 6), I expect they're not working at all, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all - they're just kids!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's what I expected. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This would include benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in the child's own name. 

For children aged 6 and 12, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm - are either of them receiving any benefits in their own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake in any type of apprenticeship?**

Given their ages (6 and 12), this would be very unlikely, but I need to confirm they are NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship program.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - they're far too young for apprenticeships!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, job seeking, etc.)
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad or in certain qualifying countries

Do you normally live in the UK, or does one of the other situations apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for both Alex and Blake?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with the child** (most common situation)
2. You contribute to their upkeep by paying at least the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent) towards their care - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do you live with Alex and Blake, or do you support them in another way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake lives with me at home. But Alex doesn't live with me - I help out with some money for clothes and things like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important information.

**For Blake (who lives with you):** That clearly meets the responsibility requirement.

**For Alex (who doesn't live with you):** The key question is whether your financial contributions are sufficient. To meet the responsibility requirement through financial support, you need to contribute **at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment** towards Alex's upkeep.

Let me get the current Child Benefit rates for you:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see the specific rates aren't listed in my reference materials, but currently (as of 2024), Child Benefit is approximately:
- **£25.60 per week** for the eldest or only child
- **£16.95 per week** for each additional child

So for Alex, you would need to be contributing at least around **£25.60 per week** (or about £110 per month) in support - this can include money for clothes, food, pocket money, housing contributions, or presents.

**Can you confirm:**
1. Do you contribute at least this amount (around £25-26 per week or £110 per month) towards Alex's upkeep?
2. If yes, how often do you make these contributions (weekly, monthly, or as lump sums)?

This will help me determine if you meet the responsibility requirement for Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I give about £20 a week for Alex - for clothes, school dinners, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is an important detail.

Based on what you've told me, you contribute **£20 per week** towards Alex's upkeep, but the requirement to be considered "responsible" through financial contributions is that you pay **at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment** - which is approximately **£25.60 per week** for the eldest child.

Since your contribution of £20 per week is below the Child Benefit amount, this unfortunately **does not meet the responsibility requirement** for Alex through financial contributions alone.

Let me clarify a few things to make sure I have the complete picture:

1. **Does Alex live with you at all** - even part-time? (For example, does Alex stay with you on weekends or during holidays?)

2. **Is there anyone else who Alex lives with** who might be claiming or could claim Child Benefit for Alex?

This is important because:
- For **Blake** (who lives with you), you clearly meet the responsibility requirement ✓
- For **Alex**, we need to determine if you can claim based on your current arrangement

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me at all - he lives with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is helpful information.

So to summarize:
- **Alex (12 years old)** lives with your ex-partner, and you contribute £20 per week towards his upkeep
- **Blake (6 years old)** lives with you

Since Alex lives with your ex-partner full-time and your financial contribution (£20/week) is below the Child Benefit threshold (approximately £25.60/week), you would not meet the responsibility requirement for Alex. Your ex-partner, who Alex lives with, would be the one who could claim Child Benefit for Alex.

However, **you do meet the responsibility requirement for Blake** since Blake lives with you.

Let me continue with the assessment for Blake. I need to check a few more things:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake specifically:**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or is there anyone else who might also want to claim for Blake (such as another parent or guardian)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake lives with me, but their mum already claims Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - this is an important piece of information. Blake's mum is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

**Here's the situation:**

Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any given time. Since Blake's mum is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, there are a few possible scenarios:

1. **If you and Blake's mum agree** that you should be the one claiming instead (because Blake lives with you), the claim can be transferred to you. She would need to stop her claim, and you would apply.

2. **If there's a dispute** about who should claim, HMRC would need to make a decision based on who has the primary responsibility for Blake.

3. **If you're both happy** with the current arrangement (her claiming even though Blake lives with you), that can continue.

**Can you tell me:**
- Are you and Blake's mum in agreement about who should claim Child Benefit for Blake?
- Do you want to claim Child Benefit for Blake, or are you happy with the current arrangement where she claims it?

This will help me determine the next steps for your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I want to claim it - Blake lives with me full-time now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. This is important.

Since Blake lives with you full-time and you want to claim Child Benefit for Blake, but Blake's mum is currently claiming, I need to understand the situation better:

**Has Blake's mum agreed to transfer the claim to you?** 

In other words:
- Does she agree that you should now be the one claiming Child Benefit since Blake lives with you full-time?
- Or is this something you haven't discussed yet, or is there disagreement about it?

This matters because:
- **If she agrees** to stop her claim and transfer it to you, the process is straightforward
- **If there's disagreement or dispute** about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the decision

What's the situation between you and Blake's mum regarding this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We haven't really sorted it out yet - there's a bit of a disagreement about it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being honest about that. This is actually a situation that HMRC needs to handle directly.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, let me give you a complete assessment:

---

## **Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment**

### **For Alex (12 years old):**
**You are NOT eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**

**Reasons:**
- Alex does not live with you
- Your financial contribution (£20/week) is below the required threshold (approximately £25.60/week) to meet the "responsibility" requirement through financial support alone
- Alex lives with your ex-partner, who would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for Alex

---

### **For Blake (6 years old):**
**Your eligibility is DEFERRED - HMRC needs to decide**

**Reasons:**
- Blake lives with you full-time, which means you meet the responsibility requirement
- However, Blake's mum is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- You and Blake's mum have not reached an agreement about who should claim
- When there's a dispute between two people who both have responsibility for a child, **HMRC must make the final decision**

---

## **What You Need to Do Next:**

### **For Blake:**

**Contact the Child Benefit Office** to notify them of the situation. HMRC will consider:

1. **Who Blake lives with most of the time** (primary residence) - this is you, as Blake lives with you full-time
2. **Who contributes most to Blake's upkeep** 
3. **Blake's best interests**
4. **Continuity of existing arrangements**

**How to contact them:**
- **Phone:** 0300 200 3100 (Monday to Friday, 8am to 6pm)
- **Online:** Through your Government Gateway account
- **Reference:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-child-lives-with-someone-else

**What to prepare:**
- Evidence that Blake lives with you full-time (e.g., school records showing your address, GP registration, etc.)
- Details of when Blake moved in with you permanently
- Information about your financial contributions to Blake's upkeep

HMRC's decision will be binding once they review both parties' circumstances.

---

### **For Alex:**

If you want to claim Child Benefit for Alex in the future, you would need to either:
- Have Alex live with you, OR
- Increase your financial contributions to at least the Child Benefit amount (currently around £25.60/week)

---

**Do you have any questions about this assessment or the next steps you need to take?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I understand. Thank you for explaining all of that to me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're very welcome! I'm glad I could help clarify your situation.

Just to summarize the key points:
- **For Alex:** Your ex-partner (who Alex lives with) should be claiming
- **For Blake:** Contact HMRC at 0300 200 3100 to resolve the dispute about who should claim, and have evidence ready that Blake lives with you full-time

Let me now record the final assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. The assessment is now complete and recorded.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if you reach an agreement with Blake's mum about the claim, or if Alex comes to live with you - please feel free to come back and we can reassess your eligibility.

Best of luck with contacting HMRC about Blake's situation. Since Blake lives with you full-time, that should work in your favor when HMRC makes their decision.

Take care!

reasonings: Alex (12 years old) does not qualify because: (1) Alex does not live with the claimant, and (2) the claimant's financial contribution of £20 per week is below the required threshold of approximately £25.60 per week to meet the responsibility requirement through financial support alone. Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, who would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (6 years old) - eligibility is DEFERRED to HMRC for determination. While Blake lives with the claimant full-time (meeting the responsibility requirement), Blake's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and there is a disagreement between the parties about who should claim. Under the rules, when multiple people claim responsibility for a child and cannot agree, HMRC must make the binding decision based on factors including primary residence, contribution to upkeep, and the child's best interests. The claimant must contact the Child Benefit Office with evidence of Blake's living arrangements.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is inquiring about two children: Alex (12) and Blake (6). For Alex, the claimant is not eligible because Alex lives with the ex-partner and financial contributions (£20/week) fall short of the responsibility threshold. For Blake, while the claimant meets the responsibility requirement (Blake lives with them full-time), there is a disputed claim situation as Blake's mother is already claiming Child Benefit and the parties have not reached agreement. This requires HMRC arbitration.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.reason: Multiple people claim responsibility for the child and cannot agree who should claim Child Benefit
response.guidance: When two or more people are responsible for a child and cannot agree who should claim, HMRC will make the decision. HMRC considers: (1) Who the child lives with most of the time (primary residence), (2) Who contributes most to the child's upkeep, (3) The child's best interests, (4) Continuity of existing arrangements. Both parties should contact the Child Benefit Office with evidence of their circumstances. HMRC's decision is binding. Common scenarios: separated parents with shared custody, child living part-time with each parent, grandparent vs parent both contributing.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (12 years old) does not qualify because: (1) Alex does not live with the claimant, and (2) the claimant's financial contribution of £20 per week is below the required threshold of approximately £25.60 per week to meet the responsibility requirement through financial support alone. Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, who would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (6 years old) - eligibility is DEFERRED to HMRC for determination. While Blake lives with the claimant full-time (meeting the responsibility requirement), Blake's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and there is a disagreement between the parties about who should claim. Under the rules, when multiple people claim responsibility for a child and cannot agree, HMRC must make the binding decision based on factors including primary residence, contribution to upkeep, and the child's best interests. The claimant must contact the Child Benefit Office with evidence of Blake's living arrangements.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is inquiring about two children: Alex (12) and Blake (6). For Alex, the claimant is not eligible because Alex lives with the ex-partner and financial contributions (£20/week) fall short of the responsibility threshold. For Blake, while the claimant meets the responsibility requirement (Blake lives with them full-time), there is a disputed claim situation as Blake's mother is already claiming Child Benefit and the parties have not reached agreement. This requires HMRC arbitration.
